ideas.Everything is in everything (the seeds):
The principle that "**everything is in everything**" is the distinctive core of Anaxagoras's physics — his ingenious solution to the problem, posed by Parmenides, of how change and the plurality of substances are possible without anything coming from nothing. Anaxagoras held that everything in the world contains **a portion of everything else**, and that matter is infinitely divisible, being composed of infinitely many tiny **"seeds"** (*spermata*, later called "homoeomeries") of every kind of substance.

Anaxagoras accepted the Parmenidean principle that nothing can genuinely come into being from what-is-not or pass into nothing. Yet the world is full of apparent transformations: we eat bread and water, and somehow our bodies produce flesh, blood, bone, hair, and sinew. How can flesh come from bread if nothing comes from nothing? Anaxagoras's answer is that the flesh, blood, and bone were **already present** — in tiny portions — in the bread all along; nourishment merely extracts and aggregates what was latent within it. Nothing new comes to be; there is only the **mixing and separating out** of ingredients that were there from the start.

Generalizing, Anaxagoras concluded that *every* substance contains a portion of *every* other substance: "in everything there is a portion of everything." A thing appears to be gold, or flesh, or water, not because it contains *only* that substance, but because that ingredient **predominates** in it — is present in the greatest proportion — while imperceptibly small portions of everything else are also there. Since matter is infinitely divisible, no matter how far you divide a thing, every portion still contains something of everything. This subtle theory preserved the eternity of the basic realities while explaining change as mere rearrangement — a distinctive and influential response to the Eleatic challenge.
ideas.Nous (Mind) as the cosmic ordering princ:
Anaxagoras's most celebrated and historically consequential idea is his introduction of **Nous** — "**Mind**" or "Intelligence" — as the cosmic principle that sets the primordial mixture of all things in motion and **orders** the universe. In an age of material explanations, Anaxagoras's appeal to Mind as the source of cosmic order was a striking innovation that earned him admiration from Aristotle and, more ambivalently, from Plato, and it stands as a landmark in the history of thought.

Anaxagoras pictured the original state of the cosmos as an inert, undifferentiated **mixture** in which "all things were together," with everything blended into everything and nothing distinct. What started the process that produced our ordered world? Not one of the material ingredients themselves, but **Nous** — a unique principle that Anaxagoras describes as "the finest and purest of all things," unmixed with anything else, infinite, self-ruling, and possessing knowledge and power over all. Nous initiated a great **rotation** or vortex in the primal mixture, and this whirling motion caused the ingredients to begin separating out — the dense from the rare, the hot from the cold — gradually generating the differentiated cosmos of distinct things.

The introduction of Mind as an ordering, governing principle — something more than blind matter and mechanical force — was profoundly significant, seeming to point toward a *purposive*, intelligent cause of cosmic order. Yet it also famously **disappointed** later thinkers. Both Socrates (as reported in Plato's *Phaedo*) and Aristotle complained that, having introduced Mind, Anaxagoras made frustratingly little use of it — invoking Nous only to start the initial motion and then reverting to purely mechanical, material explanations, rather than showing how Mind arranges everything *for the best*. Still, the very idea of a cosmic intelligence ordering the world was a pivotal contribution.
ideas.Naturalistic explanations of celestial b:
Anaxagoras was a bold and rigorous **naturalist** about the heavens, offering physical, materialist explanations of celestial phenomena that were remarkably advanced for his time — and, in one famous case, dangerous enough to get him prosecuted for **impiety**. Where popular belief and traditional religion regarded the sun, moon, and stars as divine beings, Anaxagoras insisted they were merely **material objects** subject to the same natural laws as things on earth.

Most notoriously, Anaxagoras declared that the **sun** is not a god but a huge, white-hot **stone** or mass of fiery metal — "a red-hot mass" or glowing rock — larger, he estimated, than the Peloponnese. The **stars**, likewise, are fiery stones, and the heavens are made of stones whirled around by the cosmic rotation and held aloft by the speed of their motion (a stone falling from the sky — a meteorite that fell at Aegospotami — was taken as dramatic confirmation). This demotion of the sun from a deity to a mere incandescent rock was profoundly shocking to Athenian religious sensibilities and contributed to the charge of impiety that eventually forced Anaxagoras to flee Athens.

Anaxagoras also gave a correct **naturalistic account of the moon**: he understood that the moon does not shine by its own light but **reflects the light of the sun** ("it is the sun that puts brightness into the moon"), and he used this insight to give the correct explanation of **eclipses** — a solar eclipse occurs when the moon passes between the earth and the sun, and a lunar eclipse when the earth's shadow falls on the moon. He recognized the moon has a surface with features like the earth's. These rational, physical explanations of celestial phenomena, stripping the heavens of divinity and subjecting them to natural law, mark Anaxagoras as a pioneer of scientific astronomy.

works.On Nature (fragments):
*On Nature* (*Peri Physeōs*) is the title of the prose treatise in which Anaxagoras set out his philosophical and cosmological system — a work, like those of most Presocratics, that survives only in **fragments** and reports, though enough remains (quoted especially by the later commentator Simplicius) to reconstruct the essentials of his thought. Written in prose in the Ionian tradition of natural inquiry, it was said to have been available for purchase in Athens, where Anaxagoras was the first major philosopher to teach, influencing figures in the circle of Pericles.

The surviving fragments reveal a system designed, like those of his contemporaries Empedocles and the atomists, to answer the challenge of **Parmenides** — to explain change and multiplicity while denying that anything comes from nothing. The treatise expounds Anaxagoras's distinctive answer: the doctrine that in the beginning "**all things were together**" in an infinite, undifferentiated mixture, and that **everything contains a portion of everything else**, so that matter is infinitely divisible into "seeds" of every kind. Things appear to be what **predominates** in them. Change is thus never genuine generation but only the separating-out and recombining of pre-existing ingredients.

The fragments also present Anaxagoras's most famous doctrine: the introduction of **Nous** (Mind) as the pure, unmixed, all-knowing cosmic principle that sets the primal mixture rotating and thereby orders the universe. And they contain his strikingly naturalistic **cosmology and astronomy** — the account of the heavenly bodies as material things, the sun as a fiery stone, the moon reflecting the sun's light, and the correct explanation of eclipses. Through these precious fragments, *On Nature* transmits one of the most original and influential systems of Presocratic philosophy.
